category_title: Conscientious ELL student who views reading as a practical tool
minimal_context: The student is looking at a daily large packet for his physical science class that he needs to complete outside of the classroom.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Here is the daily large packet for physical science that you need to complete outside of the classroom.

STUDENT: I am willing to put forth the effort required, even if it challenges me.

TUTOR: Okay, go ahead and start working on it.

STUDENT: But this is a lot of work to do outside of the classroom.

TUTOR: Just keep trying to get through the pages.

STUDENT: I am really frustrated with teachers who assign a lot of work outside of the classroom. I often fail the classes in which too much work is assigned.

options.A: Refrain from assigning significantly large packets of work outside the classroom to provide a feeling of accomplishment, gradually increasing the assignment amount as the physical science class progresses.
options.B: Combine photo elicitation with life-story splicing of narrative inquiry, having the student share photographs and stories about their family during the physical science class.
options.C: Conduct careful pre-intervention functional assessments to identify relationships between the environment and the student's behavior regarding the physical science packets outside the classroom.
options.D: Confront the student's misbehavior and indifference regarding the large physical science packets with stern lectures about the importance of completing work outside the classroom.
